original_abstract:
In this study, a library of novel bis-Schiff base derivatives incorporating a thiobarbituric acid scaffold was synthesized via a multi-step reaction pathway as part of an effort to identify potent anti-diabetic agents. The structures of the synthesized compounds were elucidated using HR-ESI-MS and NMR ($^{1}\text{H}$ and $^{13}\text{C}$) spectroscopy. All derivatives were evaluated for their *in vitro* $\alpha$-glucosidase inhibitory activity, with the majority demonstrating superior potency compared to the clinical standard, acarbose ($\text{IC}_{50} = 0.27 \pm 0.04\ \mu\text{M}$). Notably, compounds **8** ($\text{IC}_{50} = 0.10 \pm 0.05\ \mu\text{M}$) and **9** ($\text{IC}_{50} = 0.13 \pm 0.03\ \mu\text{M}$), featuring electron-withdrawing chlorine substituents, emerged as the most effective inhibitors. 

To explore the structure-activity relationships and molecular mechanisms of inhibition, molecular docking simulations were conducted, revealing strong binding affinities and key stabilizing interactions within the enzyme's active site. Furthermore, density functional theory (DFT) calculations at the B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) level were employed to analyze the electronic properties and geometric stability of the hybrids. The existence of intramolecular hydrogen bonding was confirmed through Reduced Density Gradient (RDG) and DFT-D3 analysis, while Frontier Molecular Orbital (FMO) analysis and Molecular Electrostatic Potential (MEP) mapping provided insights into the chemical reactivity and nucleophilic/electrophilic sites of the compounds. These findings suggest that these thiobarbituric acid-based bis-Schiff bases serve as promising lead scaffolds for the development of advanced therapeutic agents for the management of type 2 diabetes.
humanized_abstract:
A new set of bis-Schiff base analogues incorporating a thiobarbituric acid moiety were synthesized and investigated towards their applications in hypoglycemic agents. The derivatives were synthesized through a multistep methodology and identified via various spectroscopic tools (HR-ESI-MS, $^{1}\text{H}$ NMR and $^{13}\text{C}$ NMR). They were subsequently evaluated towards *in vitro* $\alpha$-glucosidase inhibition. Except one of the compounds, rest of the analogues exhibited better $\text{IC}_{50}$ value compared with the well-known pharmaceutical agent acarbose ($\text{IC}_{50} = 0.27 \pm 0.04\ \mu\text{M}$). Out of all synthesized derivatives compounds **8** ($\text{IC}_{50} = 0.10 \pm 0.05\ \mu\text{M}$) and **9** ($\text{IC}_{50} = 0.13 \pm 0.03\ \mu\text{MU}$) showed relatively more inhibitory activity that bears chlorine atoms which is negatively charged functional group thereby suggests positive correlation between $0$th order hardness and $\alpha$-glucosidase inhibitory activity.

Structure and activity relationships along with potential molecular targets of synthesized hybrids were investigated via molecular docking studies and observed strong binding affinities along with several critical interactions located at the active sites of enzymes. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations have been applied at B3LYP/6-311++G(d,p) level to obtain insight about molecular hybrids electronically and stably, by means of Reduced Density Gradient (RDG) and DFT-D3 along with intramolecular hydrogen bonding. In addition, FMO analysis and MEP map were made to express the reactivity behaviors of hybrids along with identification of the nucleophilic and electrophilic attacks sites. In-sights showed that thiobarbituric acid-based bis-Schiff bases may be considered as very promising lead compounds for design of more effective drugs towards type 2 diabetes.

document.input:
In this study, a library of novel bis-Schiff base derivatives incorporating a thiobarbituric acid scaffold was synthesized via a multi-step reaction pathway as part of an effort to identify potent anti-diabetic agents. The structures of the synthesized compounds were elucidated using HR-ESI-MS and NMR ($^{1}\text{H}$ and $^{13}\text{C}$) spectroscopy. To explore the structure-activity relationships and molecular mechanisms of inhibition, molecular docking simulations were conducted, revealing strong binding affinities and key stabilizing interactions within the enzyme's active site. These findings suggest that these thiobarbituric acid-based bis-Schiff bases serve as promising lead scaffolds for the development of advanced therapeutic agents for the management of type 2 diabetes.
